sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient presents with a non-healing foot ulcer on the dorsal aspect of the lateral right foot, which has been present for approximately one year.
The patient reports that the ulcer initially started as a small blister, became red and infected, and was treated with antibiotics (Keflex) five months ago.
The patient states that the ulcer has not improved despite treatment, and there is currently a significant amount of drainage.
The patient also reports a lack of feeling in the foot and some tiredness.

The patient's primary care physician (PCP) has been managing the patient's type 2 diabetes, but the patient's A1c is currently high at 10%, and the patient is still experiencing blood sugars in the 200s.
Past medical history: The patient has type 2 diabetes and has been taking Keflex (dose and frequency not specified) for an infection related to the foot ulcer.
Review of systems: The patient reports a lack of feeling in the foot and some tiredness.
Current medications: Keflex (dose and frequency not specified) for infection.
Objective:
Vital signs: Not mentioned.
Physical exam: The patient has a 1x2 cm circular wound on the dorsal aspect of the lateral right foot, proximal to the fifth MTP joint.
The wound has yellow slough and minimal granulation tissue, with no surrounding erythema or cellulitis, and no evidence of fluid collection, necrosis, or bony exposure.
The wound does not hurt when touched.
Test Results: X-ray of the right foot showed no evidence of osteomyelitis.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a diabetic foot ulcer with a history of infection and possible neuropathy.
Problem list: Diabetic foot ulcer; neuropathy; uncontrolled diabetes.
Plan:
I prescribed Colaganase ointment to be applied once daily to the wound, covered with a sterile dressing.
I will perform a debridement of the wound in the office.
I ordered an ABI (ankle-brachial index) to assess blood supply to the foot.
I prescribed a surgical shoe to be worn to reduce pressure on the area.
I will see the patient back in two weeks to assess progress and possible perform another debridement.
Follow-up: I will see the patient in two weeks to assess progress and possible perform another debridement.
